column2: Under the Foundations Law DIFC Law No. 3 of 2018, what is the maximum fine for failing to provide a copy of an assignment of rights to the Registered Agent or Registrar within 30 days?
column3: "The maximum fine for failing to provide a copy of an assignment of rights to the Registered Agent or Registrar within 30 days is $1,500. This is based on Article 10(7) of the Foundations Law, which specifies a fine for a person failing to provide a copy of assignment of rights."

column2: Under the Operating Law - DIFC Law No. 7 of 2018, who is responsible for appointing and dismissing the Registrar?
column3: "Under the Operating Law, the Board of Directors of the DIFCA is responsible for appointing and dismissing the Registrar. Specifically, the Board of Directors of the DIFCA shall appoint an individual to serve as the Registrar and may dismiss such a person from the office of Registrar. However, prior to appointing or dismissing the Registrar, the Board of Directors of the DIFCA must consult the President."
column5: "Under the Operating Law, the Board of Directors of the DIFCA is responsible for appointing and dismissing the Registrar, but it must consult the President prior to doing so."
column7: "The Board of Directors of the DIFCA is responsible for appointing and dismissing the Registrar."

column2: Review the full case files for DEC 001/2025 and TCD 001/2024 — did any judge preside over proceedings in both cases?
According to the Conclusion section of case CA 009/2024, how did the Court of Appeal rule, and what costs were awarded?
column3: "According to the Conclusion section of case CA 009/2024, the Court of Appeal allowed the appeal in part. It restored the Second Part 50 order in its amended form against Ms Onita and dismissed the appeal against the Order insofar as it related to Mr Oron. The Court accepted the second ground of appeal, finding the Judge was wrong in his approach to and interpretation of the rule and in finding Ms Onita was not an Officer. Regarding costs, the Appellant succeeded substantially in establishing the principles and the Judge's error concerning Ms Onita but failed regarding the other target. Consequently, the Appellant is in principle entitled to its costs with a measure of discount, as there is a substantial disparity in the costs claimed by the parties."
column5: "The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal in part, restoring the amended Second Part 50 order against Ms. Onita and dismissing the appeal as it related to Mr. Oron. The court found the judge was wrong in his approach and interpretation of the rule regarding Ms. Onita. On costs, the court ruled the appellant was in principle entitled to its costs but with a discount, as it succeeded substantially against Ms. Onita but failed against the other target (Mr. Oron). The appellant's claimed costs were USD 121,082.50, and the respondents' were USD 38,000, with the court noting a substantial disparity partly justified by the appellant's extensive research."
column7: "The Court of Appeal ruled to allow the Appeal to the extent of restoring the Second Part 50 order in its amended form against Ms. Onita and dismissed the Appeal against the Order concerning Mr. Oron. The Appellant was entitled to its costs with a measure of discount due to the substantial disparity in costs incurred by both parties, with the Appellant's total bill amounting to USD 121,082.50 compared to the Respondents' USD 38,000."

What is the minimum period for which a DIFC-incorporated General Partnership and a DIFC-incorporated Limited Liability Partnership must preserve their accounting records?
column3: "Based on the context, a DIFC-incorporated General Partnership must preserve its accounting records for at least six (6) years from the date of their creation, as per Article 18(2)(b) of the General Partnership Law. Similarly, a DIFC-incorporated Limited Liability Partnership must preserve its accounting records for at least six (6) years from the date of their creation, as per Article 25(3)(b) of the Limited Liability Partnership Law. Both laws specify this as the minimum period, subject to any other period that may be prescribed in the Regulations."
column5: "A DIFC-incorporated Limited Liability Partnership must preserve its accounting records for at least six years from the date they were created, unless a different period is prescribed in the Regulations. The context does not provide information regarding the preservation period for accounting records of a DIFC-incorporated General Partnership."
column7: "Both a DIFC-incorporated General Partnership and a DIFC-incorporated Limited Liability Partnership must preserve their accounting records for at least six (6) years from the date upon which they were created."
